planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; stinking hellebore is the most shade-tolerant and most distinctly colored of the commonly grown hellebores, producing in midwinter through early spring nodding, bell-shaped, pale chartreuse-green flowers with a maroon rim at the petal margin. Unlike most hellebores, it is fully evergreen with handsome, deeply divided, dark-green palmate leaves that persist year-round. Self-seeds freely in suitable conditions. The unpleasant foliage odor when crushed gives it the common name.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; stinking hellebore is the most architecturally distinctive and most unusually colored of all the hellebores — a robust, fully evergreen shade perennial native to woodland edges, rocky limestone slopes, and scrub across western and central Europe from Britain and France through the Mediterranean region. The deeply divided, palmate, dark-green, leathery leaves are arranged in a dramatic, spreading, star-like pattern on erect stems that persist through the full year — giving the plant a bold, almost tropical-looking architectural foliage presence in the shade garden throughout all twelve months. In midwinter through early spring (January through April in zone 9), the erect stems produce terminal clusters of numerous small, nodding, bell-shaped flowers of an unusual, distinctive pale chartreuse-green color, each flower tipped with a thin, dark maroon-purple rim at the petal margin that creates a distinctive two-toned, green-and-maroon effect quite unlike the warm pinks, purples, and creams of H. orientalis hybrids. The plant self-seeds freely under suitable conditions — in sheltered gardens with consistent moisture and part shade it can naturalize into spreading colonies. The foliage emits an unpleasant odor when bruised, hence the common names 'stinking' hellebore and 'bear's foot' (for the palmate leaf shape). One of the best winter-flowering shade perennials for zone 5–9 gardens with a preference for alkaline soil.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in part shade; Corsican hellebore is the largest and most boldly textured of the evergreen hellebores — a robust, shrubby plant with strongly three-lobed, leathery, spiny-edged leaves and large, open clusters of pale apple-green, cup-shaped flowers in late winter through spring. Native to Corsica and Sardinia. The stems are biennial: they flower in their second year then die back to the crown, which produces new stems continuously. More sun-tolerant than most hellebores.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in part shade; Corsican hellebore is the most robust, most boldly architectural, and largest-foliaged of the hellebores — a native of the rocky hillsides and scrub woodlands of Corsica and Sardinia that grows in cultivation as a dense, shrubby mound of strongly three-parted, leathery, dark blue-green leaves with spiny-toothed margins on stout, erect biennial stems (stems flower in their second year then die to the base; the plant is perennial through the continuous replacement of stems from the rootstock). The bold, spiny, three-lobed leaves give it an architectural foliage texture without parallel among the hellebores — the overall impression, particularly of a mature clump with multiple erect stems of 2–3 feet, is of a substantial, bold, textural shade or part-shade structural plant of considerable presence throughout the full year. In late winter and early spring, the robust stems terminate in large, branched clusters carrying numerous cup-shaped flowers of a characteristic pale, fresh, apple-green to yellow-green color — open-faced, wide, and much more visible than the nodding flowers of H. orientalis hybrids. The pale, fresh-green flower clusters against the dark, spiny, architectural foliage create an elegant late-winter composition. More tolerant of sun and dryness than most hellebores. An outstanding structural shade and part-shade plant for zones 6–9.

subcategory: most cold-hardy begonia, shade summer-fall bloomer

planting.note: Plant small tubers or transplants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade after last frost; hardy begonia is the most cold-hardy begonia species, surviving winter as tuberous roots to zone 6. It produces pink (or white in cultivar 'Alba') pendulous flower clusters from midsummer through fall. The plant produces small aerial bulbils in the leaf axils in late summer; these fall and naturalize freely in moist shade gardens. Leaves have characteristic begonia asymmetry with red undersides.

directions: Plant small tubers or transplants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade after last frost; hardy begonia is the most cold-hardy member of the enormous Begonia genus — a native of eastern Asia from China to Japan that survives winter as a dormant tuberous root to USDA zone 6, making it a true perennial shade plant in the great majority of North American gardens where all other begonia species are annuals or tender houseplants. It emerges from the soil in late spring and by midsummer has formed an attractive, upright clump of the characteristic one-sided, asymmetrical, heart-shaped begonia leaves with a green upper surface and red-flushed underside, the whole texture giving the shaded area a lush, exotic, tropical-feeling richness. From midsummer through autumn it produces pendulous, branched clusters of cheerful, rose-pink (or pure white in the cultivar 'Alba') single begonia flowers — the typical round, four-petaled begonia form, the smaller male flowers with their prominent yellow stamens contrasting with the slightly larger female flowers in the same cluster. The plant naturalizes readily by producing numerous small, rice-grain-sized aerial bulbils (tiny plantlets) in the leaf axils in late summer; when these fall and land in suitable moist shade, they establish freely, gradually forming expanding naturalized colonies. One of the most reliable and satisfying summer-fall-blooming shade perennials for zones 6–9 east of the Rockies.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; poor man's rhododendron is a large, shrubby, perennial impatiens from the highlands of East Africa that can reach 4–6 feet in mild climates and blooms nearly continuously with large, lavender-pink to white, spurred flowers throughout the warmer months. In zone 9 it grows as a reliable perennial. Prefers consistent moisture and shade to part shade; wilts in drought or full sun. Outstanding for filling large shady areas with continuous flower color.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; poor man's rhododendron is a large, shrubby, perennial impatiens native to the cool, moist, highland forests and forest margins of East Africa (Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda), where it grows to 4–6 feet in sheltered, moist situations and blooms almost without pause through the entire growing season. The common name 'poor man's rhododendron' refers to its remarkable capacity to produce continuous, abundant flower color in shade — filling the role that rhododendrons play in cool, acid, Pacific Northwest gardens — but in a warm-climate shade garden context where rhododendrons cannot grow. The flowers are the characteristic impatiens form — five-petaled, flat-faced, with a distinctive curved nectar spur — but much larger than the common bedding impatiens (Impatiens walleriana), reaching 1.5–2 inches across, in clear lavender-pink to soft pink to pale lilac and white. They are produced in continuous succession at the stem tips throughout the warmer months, a single plant carrying dozens of open flowers simultaneously when in full growing mode. In zone 9 it grows as a genuine, long-lived perennial shrub — not an annual — developing woody base stems over multiple seasons and increasing in size to form a large, leafy mound that fills a shady corner with continuous flower color and lush, rich, tropical-looking foliage. Requires consistent moisture and protection from midday sun; wilts quickly in drought or full-sun exposure. Outstanding for large, moist, shaded spaces in Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens.

subcategory: most cold-hardy fan palm for temperate gardens

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; windmill palm is the most cold-hardy fan palm widely available, surviving to about 5–10°F (-15 to -12°C) when established. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is a reliable, permanently hardy landscape palm reaching 20–30 feet over decades. In part shade it grows more slowly but maintains its characteristic single, slender, fiber-covered trunk with spreading fan leaves. Produces fragrant yellow flower clusters in spring, followed by blue-black fruits on female plants.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; windmill palm is the most cold-hardy fan palm in general cultivation — a native of the mountain forests of central China and Japan at elevations up to 7,500 feet, where it endures significant cold, frost, and even snow on its characteristic fan-shaped leaves. The hardiness extends to approximately 5–10°F (-15 to -12°C) when the palm is established and planted in a sheltered, well-drained position, making it a genuine, permanent landscape palm for gardens as cold as zone 7b — including much of the mid-Atlantic coast, the Pacific Northwest, the British Isles, and large areas of the American Southeast where other palms cannot survive. The distinctive characteristic of the species is the single, slender trunk covered in dense, dark-brown, burlap-like fibrous matting — the remains of old leaf bases — giving it an instantly recognizable, rough-textured, shaggy appearance quite unlike the smooth, gray trunks of many tropical palms. The circular, fan-shaped, blue-green leaf blades, divided into numerous stiff, radiating segments, are held on long petioles at the top of the trunk. In spring, the palm produces arching clusters of small, fragrant, yellow flowers from among the leaf bases; female plants (the species is dioecious) subsequently produce small, blue-black, waxy fruits. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it is a completely reliable, permanently established landscape palm that grows steadily over decades. One of the defining plants of warm-climate gardening in zones 7–11.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; lady palm is one of the most shade-tolerant palms available — thriving in conditions that would stress most palms, including deep shade, indoor light levels, and enclosed patios. In zone 9 it is an outstanding landscape palm for shaded positions where other palms fail. Slow-growing but very long-lived; old clumps become multi-stemmed specimens of considerable grace. Widely used as an interior plant in subtropical and tropical regions.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; lady palm is one of the most shade-tolerant and most elegantly refined of all the palms — a multi-stemmed, clumping, fan-leafed palm of Chinese origin (native to southern China, probably Guangdong province) that has been cultivated in Japan for centuries as an esteemed garden and indoor specimen, with numerous named cultivars developed over generations of Japanese horticulture. The palm grows as a dense clump of multiple, slender, bamboo-like stems 1–1.5 inches in diameter, each covered in the distinctive dark-brown, woven-fiber leaf-base sheathing, the whole clump increasing in number of stems over the years to form a graceful, multi-stemmed mass of considerable elegance. The fan-shaped leaves are deeply divided into 5–8 broad, blunt-tipped, ridged segments of a rich, deep glossy green, arranged in a spreading, circular pattern at the tips of the slender stems. The overall effect of a mature clump — multiple slender fiber-covered stems of different heights, each topped with a circular fan of broad, deep-green leaf segments — is one of the most graceful and refined of any palm. The exceptional shade tolerance — growing well in conditions equivalent to bright indirect indoor light — makes it uniquely valuable as a landscape palm for shaded positions where most palms fail: under tree canopy, on north-facing walls, in covered outdoor rooms and patios, and in spaces where strong shade prevents the use of most ornamental plants. Slow-growing but very long-lived; established clumps in zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens persist for decades.
